1.synthesis: The second plague sends frogs from the Nile throughout Egypt — into houses, bedrooms, beds, ovens, kneading bowls, upon Pharaoh and his servants and his people (vv.3–4). The magicians replicate the sign — producing more frogs — but cannot remove them. Pharaoh, for the first time, makes a concession: he summons Moses and Aaron and asks them to pray that YHWH remove the frogs, promising to let Israel go (v.8). Moses gives Pharaoh the honor of setting the time: 'Be pleased to command me when I am to pray for you and for your servants and for your people' (v.9). Pharaoh says 'tomorrow.' Moses prays; the frogs die; Pharaoh sees relief and hardens his heart (v.15). The pattern of false repentance — concession under pressure, reversal when pressure lifts — is established at the second plague.
1.historical_context: The frog (tz'phardea, Egyptian: qrr) was the goddess Heket, depicted frog-headed, associated with fertility and childbirth. The second plague fills Egypt with its own sacred animal, turning veneration into infestation. The frogs enter every private space — beds, ovens, kneading bowls — making domestic life impossible. The magicians' duplication (v.7) is perverse: they produce more frogs but cannot remove them. Pharaoh's first concession (v.8) is significant: he acknowledges Moses and Aaron's access to divine power by asking them to intercede — the king who denied knowing YHWH (5:2) now asks to be prayed for. Moses's offer to let Pharaoh name the time of the prayer (v.9) is an act of pastoral generosity: it leaves Pharaoh with no excuse for skepticism about the miracle's divine origin.
1.literary_structure: The second plague introduces the first instance of Pharaoh's partial capitulation and rapid re-hardening — the pattern that will repeat through plagues four, seven, and ten (the 'breakthrough' plagues where Pharaoh nearly yields). The structure of vv.8–15 models the psychology of false repentance: pressure produces promise; relief dissolves promise. Matthew Henry notes this as a pattern of sinners who cry to God in affliction and forget their vows in relief. The plague's end — frogs die and pile up until the land stinks (v.14) — provides no clean conclusion: the relief of plague-removal becomes its own form of judgment.
1.theological_application: Gill on Pharaoh's promise: 'He does not say I will let them go for good and all, but only to sacrifice, and that in the wilderness, and after only three days: he was not yet come to the point, and was only moved by the extremity he was in, and in hopes of relief.' Matthew Henry: 'Now we see what the nature of a sinner's repentance in affliction is; when the rod is upon them they promise fair; but, when the rod is removed, they forget their promises.' The frog-plague establishes the theology of temporary repentance: the same sensory pressure that produces a cry to God produces, once removed, a return to the hardness that brought the judgment. Relief is not redemption.
1.christology: Pharaoh's prayer-request — 'pray to YHWH to take away the frogs' (v.8) — is the first instance of a pagan ruler appealing to the covenant God for intercession. It anticipates the pattern of the nations ultimately coming to Israel's God for deliverance. The frogs filling every space, even beds and ovens, parallel the pervasive nature of sin — it enters every domestic space and makes normal life impossible. Moses's intercession, which removes the plague on request, is a type of the mediatorial work of Christ, whose intercession removes the curse at the appointed time. Moses allows Pharaoh to set the time (v.9) as a proof of divine sovereignty: when Moses prays at the time Pharaoh names, the timing vindicates YHWH's control of the miracle.

16.synthesis: Without warning to Pharaoh, Aaron strikes the dust of the earth and gnats (kinnim) come upon all people and animals throughout Egypt (vv.16–17). The magicians attempt to replicate it and fail: 'The magicians tried by their secret arts to produce gnats, but they could not' (v.18). They deliver their verdict: 'This is the finger of God' (v.19). Pharaoh's heart is hardened, and he will not listen. The third plague marks the decisive break in the magicians' power: they can replicate water-to-blood and frog-production, but they cannot replicate the gnats. Their own testimony — 'the finger of God' — acknowledges what Pharaoh refuses to: that this confrontation has moved beyond the domain of Egyptian sacred art.
16.historical_context: The third plague (gnats or lice, kinnim) breaks the pattern of the first two by having no warning to Pharaoh — no prior announcement, no diplomatic confrontation at the Nile. The escalation strategy changes: some plagues come with warning, others without. The magicians' failure at the third plague has been interpreted as a theological threshold: Egyptian magic could work with the natural elements (water, living creatures) but could not create ex nihilo from dust. The 'finger of God' (etzba Elohim) vocabulary will reappear in Deuteronomy 9:10 — the tablets of the Ten Commandments were 'written with the finger of God.' Jesus uses the phrase in Luke 11:20: 'if it is by the finger of God that I cast out demons, then the kingdom of God has come upon you.'
16.literary_structure: The third plague's brevity (four verses) contrasts with the extended narratives of some other plagues, reflecting its primary function as a turning-point marker rather than a sustained dramatic confrontation. The magicians' concession — 'this is the finger of God' — is the most significant speech in the plague narrative to this point: Egypt's own sacred practitioners acknowledge the superior divine power at work. Yet Pharaoh ignores them. The triadic structure of the first three plagues (blood, frogs, gnats) is followed by a second triad (flies, livestock death, boils) and a third (hail, locusts, darkness) before the tenth plague stands alone.
16.theological_application: Gill on 'this is the finger of God': 'this is to be ascribed to a power superior to human, to a divine power … so long as they could do something similar, or impose upon the senses of men, and make them believe they did the like, they would not acknowledge divine omnipotence; but now they were forced to own it.' Matthew Henry: 'The magicians themselves were convinced … they were not deceived themselves though they tried to deceive others. When men's consciences yield to the evidence of God's works, though they harden their hearts against it, the truth of God's power is vindicated.' Those who cannot deny the evidence of divine power and yet refuse to bow to it occupy the most exposed position in the moral universe.
16.christology: 'The finger of God' — the same phrase for the tablets of the commandments and for Christ's exorcisms — is the signature of direct divine action without human mediation. The Egyptian magicians, in conceding the 'finger of God,' speak better than they know: they acknowledge the presence of the divine Creator whose power they have been trying to match. Jesus's exorcisms performed 'by the finger of God' (Luke 11:20) demonstrate that the kingdom of God has arrived in the same way that the third plague demonstrated YHWH's sovereign power: the domain over which enemy power claimed authority is reclaimed by the divine finger without contest.

20.synthesis: The fourth plague introduces a new element: YHWH explicitly distinguishes between Israel and Egypt. 'I will put a division between my people and your people. Tomorrow this sign shall happen' (v.23). Swarms of flies fill Egypt — 'the land was ruined' (v.24) — but Goshen is entirely exempt. Pharaoh makes his most significant concession yet, attempting a compromise: Israel may sacrifice to their God, but within Egypt. Moses refuses: their sacrifices would be 'an abomination to the Egyptians' — they sacrifice animals the Egyptians consider sacred. Pharaoh yields further: go, but not very far. Moses insists they must go three days' journey. Pharaoh agrees, asks for intercession, Moses prays, the flies depart, and Pharaoh hardens again (vv.29–32).
20.historical_context: The separation of Goshen from the plague area (v.22–23) is a new development: the first three plagues affected all Egypt including, presumably, Goshen. From the fourth plague onward, YHWH makes visible the distinction between his people and Pharaoh's. This is both pastoral (reassurance to Israel) and polemical (demonstration to Egypt that YHWH specifically protects his people). Pharaoh's compromise offer — sacrifice within Egypt — targets the theological inseparability of Israelite worship and Israelite land: true worship of YHWH cannot be held within Egypt's boundaries. The 'abomination to the Egyptians' concern (v.26) is real: Egyptians considered cattle sacred in several cult traditions; slaughtering them publicly would cause a riot.
20.literary_structure: The fourth plague is the first of the 'breakthrough' plagues where Pharaoh nearly yields, proposing compromise rather than flat refusal. His two compromise positions — sacrifice in Egypt; don't go very far — are the tactics of a negotiator who knows he is losing but refuses to fully concede. Moses's firmness in rejecting both preserves the theological integrity of the demand: partial obedience to YHWH's command is not obedience. The pattern of plague-compromise-intercession-removal-re-hardening will repeat in plagues 7 (hail) and 9-10, each time with Pharaoh's concession more significant and his reversal more catastrophic.
20.theological_application: Gill on the separation: 'by this wonderful distinction God showed that he knew his own, and would preserve them, notwithstanding they were mixed with the Egyptians, and dwelt among them.' Matthew Henry on Pharaoh's compromise: 'Pharaoh is willing to let them go and offer sacrifice … but would have them do it in the land of Egypt, not go three days' journey. Many who are not willing to be altogether Christians are yet willing to be half-Christians.' The half-measure offer is the characteristic shape of resistance to the gospel's total demand: worship here, stay near, don't fully commit. YHWH's demand is not partial but complete — 'let my people go.'
20.christology: The fly-plague's separation of Goshen from Egypt — 'I will put a distinction (peduṯ, a redemption/separation) between my people and your people' (v.23) — is the Exodus's first visible act of election made visible in judgment. The same word peduṯ (redemption/separation) appears in Psalm 111:9: 'he sent redemption to his people.' The eschatological harvest parable of Matthew 13:49 — 'the angels will come out and separate the evil from the righteous' — enacts the same divine separating-in-judgment that the fly-plague introduces. YHWH's protection of his people while judgment falls on others is not partiality but covenant faithfulness.
